title: Report With Quarto
text:
Use Quarto to combine explanation, code, tables, figures, and results in a single reproducible document. Quarto reports can be rendered to HTML for websites and review, or to PDF and Word for manuscripts and reports.
Quarto can also be used to generate Beamer slides for research presentations. Regression results, tables, and figures should preferably be generated with Quarto and exported as PDF files to improve reproducibility.
When needed, research writing can be done in Word for easier editing and commenting. In that case, tables and figures should be generated separately as PDF files and attached or inserted into the main Word document.

Practical Standard
The preferred workflow is:
Data + code -> Quarto report -> Rendered tables, figures, paper, or slides
When the data or model changes, rerender the Quarto document so the report reflects the current analysis.

Back to Research Workflow | Last post: Example Workflow

title: AI-Assisted Coding
text:
AI assistants are useful for planning, code drafting, debugging, documentation, and review. They are most useful when the project has clear context, organized files, and version control.

Recommended Pattern
Use this pattern when asking an AI assistant to help with research code:
Provide context -> Ask for a plan -> Review the plan -> Implement the plan -> Run checks -> Review output -> Commit


Good Rules

Ask for a plan before file edits.
Keep the requested task concrete.
Review all code and text before accepting it.
Rerun the analysis after edits.
Do not let the assistant invent results.
Commit only after human review.



What is a context file?


What is a plan document?


Prompts


Best practices?

title: Research Workflow Tools: Install and Resources
text:
The lab workflow depends on a small set of tools that work together in one project workspace.

Positron Or VS Code
Install Positron:
https://positron.posit.co/download.html
Positron is an IDE (integrated development environment) that combines R, Python, Quarto, and Git in one interface. It is the lab’s recommended tool for research projects. Other options include VS Code and RStudio, which can also be used with the tools introduced below.


R
Install R:
https://cloud.r-project.org/
R is useful for data cleaning, statistical analysis, and visualization, especially with packages such as tidyverse and ggplot2. If you are new to R, start with the R for Data Science book to learn the basics of R programming and data analysis.
Note that if you are already using Stata, you don’t need to learn R entirely from scratch. You can use R for specific tasks such as data wrangling, visualization, and regression models. Best learning materials for Stata users include the R for Stata Users website and the R for Stata Users book. You can also use R and Stata together in the same project workspace, using R for data manipulation and visualization, and Stata for regression models if you prefer.


Python
Install Python if needed for specific tasks such as machine learning or natural language processing:
https://www.python.org/downloads/
Python is a general-purpose programming language that is widely used in data science, web development, and application development. It has powerful libraries for data manipulation (Pandas), machine learning (scikit-learn), and natural language processing (NLTK, spaCy). If you are new to Python, start with the Python for Data Science Handbook to learn how to use Python for data analysis and machine learning.
If you are already using R, you can use Python for specific tasks that are better suited for Python, such as machine learning or natural language processing. You can also use R and Python together in the same project workspace, using R for data manipulation and visualization, and Python for machine learning or NLP tasks if you prefer. Start with Rebecca Barter’s An introduction to Python for R Users tutorial to learn how to use Python as an R user.


Git
Install Git:
https://git-scm.com/downloads
Git records changes to the project over time and makes it possible to recover earlier versions.


GitHub
Create a GitHub account (free for public repositories):
https://github.com/
GitHub provides remote backup, collaboration, issue tracking, and review workflows. You can also use GitHub Copilot for AI-assisted coding, which help you to write code faster and with fewer errors. It can be used in Positron, VS Code, and RStudio. However, it is not necessary to use GitHub Copilot if you have access to Codex or Claude Code.


Quarto
Use Quarto through Positron when possible. Quarto can also be installed separately:
https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Or you can install Quarto inside Position, RStudio, or VS Code. Quarto is useful for combining code, tables, figures, and text in a single reproducible document. It can be rendered to HTML for websites and review, or to PDF and Word for manuscripts and reports. Quarto can also be used to generate Beamer slides for research presentations. Regression results, tables, and figures should preferably be generated with Quarto and exported as PDF files to improve reproducibility. When needed, research writing can be done in Word for easier editing and commenting. In that case, tables and figures should be generated separately as PDF files and attached or inserted into the main Word document.


AI Assistants
AI assistants can be added depending on access and preference:

Codex: https://chatgpt.com/codex
GitHub Copilot: https://github.com/features/copilot
Claude Code: https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code

I used both Codex and GitHub Copilot. My ChatGPT Plus subscription gives me access to Codex, which is more powerful and flexible than GitHub Copilot. I use Codex for all my AI-assisted coding work, and I use GitHub Copilot as a backup when I am still doing my coding work. I have not used Claude Code yet, but I am interested in trying it out as well.
Codex comes with the ChatGPT Plus subscription, which costs $20 per month. GitHub Copilot is a separate subscription that costs $10 per month. If you have access to Codex through ChatGPT Plus, you may not need GitHub Copilot, but it can be a useful backup option.


How to Install Codex in Positron

Open Positron and go to the Extensions tab.
Search for “ChatGPT” and install the “ChatGPT Extension” by OpenAI.
After installation, you will see a new ChatGPT icon in the sidebar. Note that Positron has a primary sidebar and a secondary sidebar. The ChatGPT extension will be added to the secondary sidebar. You can move it to the primary sidebar if you want.
Click on the ChatGPT icon and sign in with your OpenAI account that has access to Codex through ChatGPT Plus.
Once signed in, you can start using the ChatGPT extension to get AI-assisted coding help directly within Positron.

Note that Positron also has its own AI assistant called “Positron AI,” which can be powered through ChatGPT, but you will need to connect it through your GPT API key, which costs extra money. You do not need to use Positron AI if you have access to Codex through the ChatGPT extension.
Another option for installing Codex is to install it in the terminal and use it through the command line. You can follow the instructions in the OpenAI documentation to install the OpenAI CLI and set up Codex for command-line use: https://platform.openai.com/docs/cli . This can be useful if you prefer to use Codex outside of an IDE or if you want to use it in a different environment. You can also use multiple terminals for parallel use of Codex AI agents on multiple tasks.
My video tutorial on how to install and use Codex in Positron is available here: place holder


How to install GitHub Copilot in Positron:

Open Positron and go to the Extensions tab.
Search for “GitHub Copilot” and install the “GitHub Copilot” extension by GitHub.
After installation, you will see a new GitHub Copilot icon in the sidebar.
Click on the GitHub Copilot icon and sign in with your GitHub account that has a Copilot subscription.
Once signed in, you can start using GitHub Copilot to get AI-assisted coding help directly within Positron.


Back to Research Workflow | Last post: Why We Need A New Research Workflow | Next post: Initial Set Up

title: Research Workflow
text:
The State and Local Government Finance Research Lab uses a reproducible, transparent, and reviewable workflow for research projects. The goal is to make the path from data to evidence clear enough that the work can be checked, revised, and extended.

Core Principles
Good research workflow should make each project:

Reproducible: another researcher can rerun the analysis from the project files.
Traceable: important changes are recorded in version control.
Readable: data notes, code, outputs, and interpretation are organized in one project.
Recoverable: mistakes can be corrected without losing prior work.
Collaborative: work can be shared, reviewed, and improved through GitHub.
Human-led: AI tools can assist with coding and review, but researchers remain responsible for design, interpretation, and judgment.



Recommended Toolchain
The lab’s default workflow combines:

Positron or VS Code: an integrated development environment for R, Python, Quarto, and Git.
R or Python: statistical programming languages for data cleaning, analysis, modeling, and visualization.
Quarto: reproducible reporting for combining text, code, tables, figures, and results.
Git and GitHub: version control, backup, collaboration, and publication workflows.
AI coding assistants: Codex, Claude Code, or GitHub Copilot for planning, coding support, debugging, and review.



Workflow Posts
Use these short posts to learn the details of the workflow:

Intro: Why do we need a new research workflow?
Research Workflow Tools: Install and Resources
Initial Set Up
AI-Assisted Coding
Example Workflow
Report With Quarto

title: State and Local Government Finance Research Lab
text:
State and Local Government Finance Research Lab (SLGF-Lab) is a research branch of the State and Local Government Finance (SLGF) Project at the Center for Policy Research at Rockefeller College, University at Albany SUNY. SLGF-Lab is led by Professor Gang Chen, Co-Director of the SLGF Project. The lab focuses on research and graduate teaching on state and local government budgeting and financial management, using advanced econometric methods, simulations, and large-scale datasets.
The research presented on this website is ongoing research and teaching projects that is being conducted in the lab.

Current projects
Municipal Tax Override Studies
Property Tax Overrides and Municipal Credit Ratings: Evidence from Massachusetts
A municipal tax override is a voter-approved measure that allows a city or town to temporarily raise property taxes beyond statutory limits. While overrides enable governments to generate additional revenue in response to rising public service costs, they may also undermine the purpose of statutory limits to promote fiscal sustainability. This study uses data from Massachusetts municipalities between 2003 and 2021 to examine the impact of overrides on credit ratings and future override support. Findings show that successful overrides are associated with improved credit ratings, but frequent overrides—and especially failed override attempts—can damage both credit ratings and future voter support.
Rural School Finances Studies
Examining the Impact of Legacy Costs on New York State Rural Schools
This research focuses on the growing burden of “legacy costs” (pensions, retiree healthcare, and capital project maintenance) in rural school districts in Upstate New York facing declining enrollments. These costs consume an increasing share of budgets, crowding out instructional spending and straining schools’ financial capacity. Using detailed data from New York State, this study quantifies the scale and trends of legacy costs, compares rural and non-rural districts, and assesses their impact on school budgets and operations.
Examining the Causes and Status of Fiscal Stress in Rural Schools of New York State
Rural school districts in New York face shrinking enrollments, limited tax bases, and rising fixed costs, putting them at heightened risk of fiscal stress. This study analyzes the status, causes, and consequences of fiscal stress across rural schools using comprehensive financial indicators—such as fund balance, liquidity, and debt ratios—and compares them with non-rural districts. The findings inform policymakers and educators about the drivers of fiscal stress, contributing to policy solutions aimed at reducing inequalities and improving educational equity across the state.

title: Why We Need A New Research Workflow
text:
The way people interact with computers is changing. With AI-assisted coding and vibe coding, researchers no longer need to conduct technical analysis through hand coding or manual and repetitive processes. Many tasks that used to require constant copying, pasting, formatting, and rewriting code can now be made more efficient, more replicable, and easier to document.
We are used to the traditional academic research workflow, which is fragmented across many closed systems. We search for literature and download data in a browser. We clean and analyze data in SAS, R or Stata. We export regression tables to Word and edit them there. We make slides in PowerPoint with tables copied from Word. Along the way, we repeatedly copy results, adjust formatting, rewrite code, move files, and manually track different versions of the same project in several folders. Some of us have a master file in R or Stata to document the full workflow, but even with that, many parts of the workflow still live outside the master file.
That workflow creates unnecessary repetitive actions, and it also creates opportunities for human error: outdated tables, mismatched figures, broken file paths, undocumented data steps, and results that are hard to reproduce later. We all understand the frustration of sending your work to your collaborators and having to explain to them how to replicate the results before they can further the analysis. We also understand the frustration of leaving a project and coming back a few months later with new data or a new model, only to spend time figuring out where you left off and how to replicate the latest results before continuing your work.
A modern research workflow should be more integrated. In one project workspace, we should be able to use the best tool for each task: ggplot2 in R for visualization, Python for text analysis or machine learning, Stata for regression models, and Quarto or Markdown for writing reproducible papers and slides. Instead of moving manually across disconnected software, we can connect data, code, results, writing, and presentation materials in one transparent workflow.
The workflow should also be assisted by AI tools as much as possible for repetitive work. The goal is not to replace researchers with AI. The goal is to move repetitive technical work out of the way so researchers can spend more time on research design, theory, interpretation, and communication.
Think of it as how graphic novel artists work now.The artist focuses on the story plot, character creation, sketching and inking, and choosing the color palette, while leaving the repetitive coloring work to their assistants working with automation tools.
Our research workflow should reflect this approach as well. The old model of moving manually between isolated programs was built for a different era. We now have the tools to make research more efficient, transparent, and reproducible.
Before we go on with this approach, several tools are necessary. I will explain why we need these tools for this workflow.
First, because we want to use AI-assisted coding, we need to use coding instead of interactive actions (i.e. mouse and keyboard) to finish most of the work. That means for some simple work, like naming an axis in Excel, while you can just click and enter the name of that axis in five seconds, you now will need to use code in R to label that axis.
Second, we need to use open-source software, because that can give us endless possibilities to integrate it into an IDE such as Positron or VS Code, and allow us to integrate Codex or Claude Code as well. There is no way to integrate Codex into Stata software, and you do not want to copy and paste code from the ChatGPT chat window to a Stata do-file, which is the repetitive and error-prone approach we want to avoid.
Third, we need to use version control with Git and GitHub. Because AI agents will make changes to your files directly, you do not want to bear the risk of losing your work while AI is just trying different ways to come up with the code. In that case, with Git and GitHub, you can always play with your approach until you are ready to commit, push, and merge. Anytime you regret a change, you can always go back to the previous version.
Learning these tools and getting used to them will take time and may cause frustration at the beginning. This may be an uphill learning curve because these tools require an understanding of new languages or processes. You may make mistakes, and it may feel like a waste of time. But once you get used to them, this workflow can dramatically increase your efficiency and, more importantly, make your data management and analysis work more fun. I am also convinced that this is a forward-looking way of doing research in the near future.

Back to Research Workflow | Next post: Research Workflow Tools: Install and Resources

title: Initial Set Up
text:
Each project should live in a dedicated folder and GitHub repository. The repository should include source code, Quarto reports, documentation, and non-sensitive derived outputs.
Use GitHub-first project creation when starting from scratch. Use a local-folder-first approach when existing files already need to be organized.

Everyday Git Workflow
The everyday Git workflow is simple:
Edit -> Save -> Stage -> Commit -> Push
Keep commits small and descriptive. Do not commit confidential data, credentials, or large raw files; specify these in .gitignore to prevent them from being tracked.


Project Context
Before analysis begins, create a short project context file, such as project_context.md. It should state:

the research goals,
data sources,
expected outputs,
coding standards,
validation checks,
rules for using AI assistance.

This file helps collaborators and AI assistants understand the project before editing code.

Back to Research Workflow | Last post: Research Workflow Tools: Install and Resources | Next post: AI-Assisted Coding

title: Example Workflow
text:
Use relative paths and a clear folder structure. A typical research project can use this structure:
project-name/
|-- data/
|   |-- raw/              # Original, immutable data; do not edit directly
|   |-- interim/          # Intermediate data generated during processing
|   `-- processed/        # Cleaned or transformed datasets
|-- code/
|   |-- notebooks/        # Exploratory analysis and prototyping
|   |-- scripts/          # Reusable scripts for data processing and analysis
|   |-- src/              # Core reusable project code
|   `-- tests/            # Unit tests and validation checks
|-- output/
|   |-- figures/          # Generated plots, charts, and images
|   |-- tables/           # Generated tables and result summaries
|   `-- models/           # Trained models or serialized outputs
|-- reports/
|   |-- drafts/           # Working versions of reports or papers
|   |-- final/            # Finalized reports
|   `-- presentations/    # Slides and presentation materials
|-- docs/
|   |-- plan.md           # Project goals, milestones, timeline, and tasks
|   |-- context.md        # Background, motivation, assumptions, and scope
|   `-- references.md     # Key references and supporting materials
|-- README.md             # Project overview, setup, and usage instructions
|-- .gitignore            # Exclude confidential data, credentials, and large raw files
`-- LICENSE               # Licensing terms
Keep raw data separate from cleaned data. Record cleaning decisions. Use scripts or Quarto documents that can be rerun from start to finish.

Development Cycle
Analysis should be incremental. Run the code after each major change and check:

data dimensions and variable names,
missing values and outliers,
joins and merges,
descriptive statistics,
sensitivity to alternative specifications.

Commit the project after a meaningful unit of work is complete and the output has been reviewed.
